            Word Search (LC 79)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. Words can be constructed from adjacent cells (up/down/left/right). Same cell can't be used twice.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: board =
["A", "B", "C", "E"]
["S", "F", "C", "S"]
["A", "D", "E", "E"]
word = "ABCCED"
Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: board =
["A", "B", "C", "E"]
["S", "F", "C", "S"]
["A", "D", "E", "E"]
word = "ABCB"
Output: false

        

        

            
Grid Backtracking (board 2x3, word "ABE")

            
Path: matched cells · cell being checked · mismatch / fail

            

                
 Current

                
 On path

                
 Mismatch

                
 Success

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
Board

            

                

                    
A

                    
B

                    
C

                

                

                    
D

                    
E

                    
F

                

            

            
Word: ABE — not started

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean exist(char[][] board, String word) {
    int m = board.length, n = board[0].length;
    
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            if (backtrack(board, word, i, j, 0))
                return true;
        }
    }
    return false;
}

boolean backtrack(char[][] board, String word, int r, int c, int idx) {
    if (idx == word.length()) return true;
    if (r < 0 || r >= board.length || c < 0 || c >= board[0].length)
        return false;
    if (board[r][c] != word.charAt(idx)) return false;
    
    char temp = board[r][c];
    board[r][c] = '#'; // mark visited
    
    boolean found = backtrack(board, word, r+1, c, idx+1) ||
                    backtrack(board, word, r-1, c, idx+1) ||
                    backtrack(board, word, r, c+1, idx+1) ||
                    backtrack(board, word, r, c-1, idx+1);
    
    board[r][c] = temp; // unmark (backtrack)
    return found;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n * 3^L) where L = word length | Space: O(L)

        

    


    
    

        

            Palindrome Partitioning (LC 131)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, partition s such that every substring of the partition is a palindrome. Return all possible palindrome partitionings.

            
  • 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 16
  • s contains only lowercase English letters

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "aab"
Output: [["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "a"
Output: [["a"]]

        

        

            
Partition tree (s = "aab")

            
Edges: chosen substring. Gold = partition added to result

            

                
 Current

                
 Visited edge

                
 Result node

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
            

            

                

                    
Current partition

                    

                

                

                    
Results

                    

                

            

            
Partitions found: 0

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<List<String>> partition(String s) {
    List<List<String>> result = new ArrayList<>();
    backtrack(result, new ArrayList<>(), s, 0);
    return result;
}

void backtrack(List<List<String>> result, List<String> current,
               String s, int start) {
    if (start == s.length()) {
        result.add(new ArrayList<>(current));
        return;
    }

    for (int end = start + 1; end <= s.length(); end++) {
        String sub = s.substring(start, end);
        if (isPalindrome(sub)) {
            current.add(sub);
            backtrack(result, current, s, end);
            current.remove(current.size() - 1);
        }
    }
}

boolean isPalindrome(String s) {
    int left = 0, right = s.length() - 1;
    while (left < right) {
        if (s.charAt(left++) != s.charAt(right--)) return false;
    }
    return true;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n * 2^n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Generate Parentheses (LC 22)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given n pairs of parentheses, generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.

            
  • 1 ≤ n ≤ 8

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: n = 3
Output: ["((()))","(()())","(())()","()(())","()()()"]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: n = 1
Output: ["()"]

        


        

            
Decision Tree Animation (n=2)

            
At each step, choose ( if open < n, or ) if close < open. Leaf when length = 2n.

            

                
 Current

                
 Add (

                
 Add )

                
 Valid Result

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
            

            

                

                    
Valid Parentheses Found

                    

                

            

            
n = 2 | Valid combinations: 0

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List generateParenthesis(int n) {
    List result = new ArrayList<>();
    backtrack(result, "", 0, 0, n);
    return result;
}

void backtrack(List result, String current,
               int open, int close, int n) {
    if (current.length() == 2 * n) {
        result.add(current);
        return;
    }
    
    // Can add '(' if open count < n
    if (open < n) {
        backtrack(result, current + "(", open + 1, close, n);
    }
    
    // Can add ')' only if close count < open count
    if (close < open) {
        backtrack(result, current + ")", open, close + 1, n);
    }
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(4^n / sqrt(n)) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    

    
    

        

            Merge Sort (Divide and Conquer)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Sort an array using the merge sort algorithm. Divide the array into halves, recursively sort each half, then merge them back together.

            
  • Classic divide and conquer algorithm
  • Stable sort - preserves order of equal elements

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]
Output: [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: [5, 1, 4, 2]
Output: [1, 2, 4, 5]

        

        

            
Merge sort trace — [4, 2, 1, 3]

            
Left range
             Right range
             Merge / done

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
Array state

            

            
Range: —

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
void mergeSort(int[] arr, int left, int right) {
    if (left >= right) return; // base case: single element

    int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
    mergeSort(arr, left, mid);       // sort left half
    mergeSort(arr, mid + 1, right);  // sort right half
    merge(arr, left, mid, right);    // merge sorted halves
}

void merge(int[] arr, int left, int mid, int right) {
    int[] temp = new int[right - left + 1];
    int i = left, j = mid + 1, k = 0;

    while (i <= mid && j <= right) {
        if (arr[i] <= arr[j]) temp[k++] = arr[i++];
        else temp[k++] = arr[j++];
    }

    while (i <= mid) temp[k++] = arr[i++];
    while (j <= right) temp[k++] = arr[j++];

    // Copy back
    for (int x = 0; x < temp.length; x++)
        arr[left + x] = temp[x];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log n) | Space: O(n)

        

    








    

        
7. Top K Elements (8 Questions)

        
8 heap problems + 2 concept explainers — find k largest, smallest, or most frequent elements

    


    
    

        

            Kth Largest Element in Array (LC 215)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element. It is the kth largest in sorted order, not the kth distinct.

            
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ nums.length ≤ 10^5

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k = 2
Output: 5

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [3,2,3,1,2,4,5,5,6], k = 4
Output: 4

        


        

            
Min-Heap Animation (nums=[3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2)

            

                
 Current Element

                
 In Heap

                
 Removed from Heap

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Array

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap (size k=2)

                    

                

            

            
Heap: [] | Answer: ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int findKthLargest(int[] nums, int k) {
    // Min heap of size k
    PriorityQueue minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
    
    for (int num : nums) {
        minHeap.offer(num);
        
        // Keep heap size = k
        if (minHeap.size() > k) {
            minHeap.poll(); // remove smallest
        }
    }
    
    // Top of min heap is kth largest
    return minHeap.peek();
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log k) | Space: O(k)

        

    


    
    

        

            Kth Largest Element in a Stream (LC 703)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Design a class to find the kth largest element in a stream. It is the kth largest in sorted order, not the kth distinct. Implement the KthLargest class with a constructor that accepts k and an initial array, and an add(val) method that returns the current kth largest after inserting val.

            
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ 104
  • 0 ≤ nums.length ≤ 104

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: ["KthLargest", "add", "add", "add", "add", "add"]
[[3, [4, 5, 8, 2]], [3], [5], [10], [9], [4]]
Output: [null, 4, 5, 5, 8, 8]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: ["KthLargest", "add"] [[1, []], [-3]]
Output: [null, -3]

        

        

            
Stream Add Animation (k=3, init=[4,5,8,2], add: 3,5,10)

            

                
 Current Op

                
 In Heap

                
 Removed

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Min-Heap (size k=3)

                    

                

            

            
Heap: [] | Returns: ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
class KthLargest {
    PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap;
    int k;

    KthLargest(int k, int[] nums) {
        this.k = k;
        minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
        for (int num : nums) add(num);
    }

    int add(int val) {
        minHeap.offer(val);
        // Keep heap size = k
        if (minHeap.size() > k) {
            minHeap.poll(); // remove smallest
        }
        return minHeap.peek(); // kth largest
    }
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log k) constructor, O(log k) per add | Space: O(k)

        

    


    
    

        

            Top K Frequent Elements (LC 347)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an integer array nums and integer k, return the k most frequent elements.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [1,1,1,2,2,3], k = 2
Output: [1,2]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1], k = 1
Output: [1]

        

        

            
Top K Frequent Animation (nums=[1,1,1,2,2,3], k=2)

            

                
 Current

                
 In Heap

                
 Removed

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Frequency Map

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap (by freq)

                    

                

            

            
freqMap = {} | Heap = [] | Result = ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int[] topKFrequent(int[] nums, int k) {
    // Step 1: Count frequencies
    HashMap freqMap = new HashMap<>();
    for (int num : nums)
        freqMap.put(num, freqMap.getOrDefault(num, 0) + 1);
    
    // Step 2: Min heap sorted by frequency
    PriorityQueue minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> freqMap.get(a) - freqMap.get(b)
    );
    
    for (int num : freqMap.keySet()) {
        minHeap.offer(num);
        if (minHeap.size() > k) minHeap.poll();
    }
    
    // Step 3: Build result
    int[] result = new int[k];
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) result[i] = minHeap.poll();
    return result;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log k) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Top K Frequent Words (LC 692)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an array of strings words and an integer k, return the k most frequent strings. The answer should be sorted by frequency from highest to lowest. If two words have the same frequency, the lexicographically smaller word comes first.

            
  • 1 ≤ words.length ≤ 500
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ number of unique words

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: words = ["i","love","leetcode","i","love","coding"], k = 2
Output: ["i","love"]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: words = ["the","day","is","sunny","the","the","the","sunny","is","is"], k = 4
Output: ["the","is","sunny","day"]

        

        

            
Top K Words Animation (words=["i","love","leetcode","i","love","coding"], k=2)

            

                
 Current

                
 In Heap

                
 Removed

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Frequency Map

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap (by freq/lex)

                    

                

            

            
freqMap = {} | Heap = [] | Result = ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<String> topKFrequent(String[] words, int k) {
    // Step 1: Count frequencies
    Map<String, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
    for (String w : words)
        freq.put(w, freq.getOrDefault(w, 0) + 1);

    // Step 2: Min heap — lower freq first, higher lexicographic first
    PriorityQueue<String> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> freq.get(a).equals(freq.get(b)) ?
            b.compareTo(a) : freq.get(a) - freq.get(b)
    );

    for (String w : freq.keySet()) {
        minHeap.offer(w);
        if (minHeap.size() > k) minHeap.poll();
    }

    // Step 3: Build result in reverse
    List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
    while (!minHeap.isEmpty())
        result.add(0, minHeap.poll());
    return result;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log k) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Sort Characters By Frequency (LC 451)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, sort it in decreasing order based on the frequency of the characters. If two characters have the same frequency, their order doesn't matter. Return the sorted string.

            
  • 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 5 × 105
  • s consists of uppercase/lowercase English letters and digits

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "tree"
Output: "eert" (or "eetr")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "cccaaa"
Output: "aaaccc" (or "cccaaa")

        

        

            
Frequency Sort Animation (s = "tree")

            

                
 Current

                
 Added to Result

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Frequency Map

                    

                

                

                    
Max-Heap

                    

                

            

            
Result: ""

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
String frequencySort(String s) {
    // Step 1: Count character frequencies
    Map<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
    for (char c : s.toCharArray())
        freq.put(c, freq.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);

    // Step 2: Max heap by frequency
    PriorityQueue<Character> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> freq.get(b) - freq.get(a)
    );
    maxHeap.addAll(freq.keySet());

    // Step 3: Build result string
    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
        char c = maxHeap.poll();
        for (int i = 0; i < freq.get(c); i++)
            sb.append(c);
    }
    return sb.toString();
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            K Closest Points to Origin (LC 973)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an array of points where points[i] = [xi, yi] represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer k, return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0). Distance is Euclidean: √(x² + y²).

            
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ points.length ≤ 104

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: points = [[1,3],[-2,2]], k = 1
Output: [[-2,2]]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: points = [[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k = 2
Output: [[3,3],[-2,4]]

        

        

            
K Closest Points Animation (points=[[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k=2)

            

                
 Current Point

                
 In Heap

                
 Removed (farthest)

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Points (dist²)

                    

                

                

                    
Max-Heap (by dist)

                    

                

            

            
Heap = [] | Result = ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int[][] kClosest(int[][] points, int k) {
    // Max heap by distance (keep k smallest)
    PriorityQueue<int[]> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> (b[0]*b[0] + b[1]*b[1])
                 - (a[0]*a[0] + a[1]*a[1])
    );

    for (int[] p : points) {
        maxHeap.offer(p);
        // If heap exceeds k, remove farthest
        if (maxHeap.size() > k) {
            maxHeap.poll();
        }
    }

    return maxHeap.toArray(new int[0][]);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log k) | Space: O(k)

        

    


    
    

        

            💡 Understanding Distance Calculation: How is d² calculated?
            Concept Note
        

        

            
            

                
❓ The Question:

                

                    "In the K Closest Points to Origin problem, I see values like [3,3] has d² = 18. I don't understand the math behind it. How is this calculated?"
                

            

            
            

                
📐 The Formula: d² = x² + y²

                
Distance from point (x, y) to origin (0, 0) = √(x² + y²). We use d² = x² + y² (skip √) because we only need to compare distances.

                
                

                    

                        

                            Point [x, y]
                            Calculation
                            d²
                        

                        

                            [3, 3]
                            3² + 3² = 9 + 9
                            18
                        

                        

                            [5, -1]
                            5² + (-1)² = 25 + 1
                            26
                        

                        

                            [-2, 4]
                            (-2)² + 4² = 4 + 16
                            20
                        

                    

                

            


            

                
💭 Key Points:

                
    
                    
  • Formula: d² = x² + y²
    • 
                    
  • Negative values: Squaring makes them positive → (-2)² = 4
    • 
                    
  • Why skip √? Only comparing distances, not calculating exact values
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        

            Merge K Sorted Lists (LC 23)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Given k sorted linked-lists, merge all into one sorted linked-list and return it.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: lists = [[1,4,5],[1,3,4],[2,6]]
Output: [1,1,2,3,4,4,5,6]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: lists = []
Output: []

        


        

            
Merge K Lists Animation (lists=[[1,4,5],[1,3,4],[2,6]])

            

                
 Current Min

                
 In Heap

                
 Merged

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Lists

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap

                    

                

            

            
Merged: []

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
ListNode mergeKLists(ListNode[] lists) {
    if (lists == null || lists.length == 0) return null;
    
    // Min heap by node value
    PriorityQueue minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> a.val - b.val
    );
    
    // Add first node of each list
    for (ListNode node : lists) {
        if (node != null) minHeap.offer(node);
    }
    
    ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0);
    ListNode current = dummy;
    
    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
        ListNode minNode = minHeap.poll();
        current.next = minNode;
        current = current.next;
        
        // Add next node from same list
        if (minNode.next != null) minHeap.offer(minNode.next);
    }
    
    return dummy.next;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(N log k) | Space: O(k)

        

    


    
    

        

            Kth Smallest Element in M Sorted Arrays
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given M sorted arrays and an integer k, find the kth smallest element among all elements across all arrays. Each array is sorted in ascending order.

            
  • 1 ≤ M ≤ 100
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ total number of elements
  • Each array is sorted in ascending order

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: arrays = [[1,5,9],[2,3,15],[4,8,12]], k = 4
Output: 4 (sorted: 1,2,3,4,5,8,9,12,15)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: arrays = [[10,20],[5,15]], k = 3
Output: 15 (sorted: 5,10,15,20)

        

        

            
Kth Smallest Animation (arrays=[[1,5,9],[2,3,15],[4,8,12]], k=4)

            

                
 Current Min

                
 In Heap

                
 Extracted

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Arrays

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap

                    

                

            

            
count = 0 | Answer = ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int kthSmallest(int[][] arrays, int k) {
    // Min heap: {value, arrayIndex, elementIndex}
    PriorityQueue<int[]> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]
    );

    // Add first element of each array
    for (int i = 0; i < arrays.length; i++) {
        if (arrays[i].length > 0)
            minHeap.offer(new int[]{arrays[i][0], i, 0});
    }

    int count = 0;
    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
        int[] curr = minHeap.poll();
        count++;
        // If this is the kth element, return it
        if (count == k) return curr[0];

        int arrIdx = curr[1], eleIdx = curr[2];
        // Add next element from same array
        if (eleIdx + 1 < arrays[arrIdx].length) {
            minHeap.offer(new int[]{
                arrays[arrIdx][eleIdx + 1], arrIdx, eleIdx + 1
            });
        }
    }
    return -1;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(k log M) | Space: O(M)

        

    


    
    

        

            💡 Why Not Dump Entire Arrays into Heap?
            Concept Note
        

        

            
            

                
❓ The Question:

                

                    "For problems like Merge K Sorted Lists (LC 23) and Kth Smallest in M Sorted Arrays, can we not take one whole array at once, empty all its elements into the min-heap, then take the next array and do the same?"
                

            

            
            

                
❌ Why This Doesn't Work

                
If you dump ALL elements from ALL arrays into heap:

                
    
                    
  • Huge heap size: O(n×m) space (n = arrays, m = avg size) instead of O(k)
    • 
                    
  • Lose sorted advantage: Not using the fact each array is already sorted
    • 
                    
  • Inefficient: O(n×m log(n×m)) time instead of O(n×m log k)
    • 
                    
  • Defeats the purpose: You're essentially sorting everything from scratch!
    • 
                

            


            

                
✓ The Smart Approach: One Element Per Array

                
Key Insight: Since each array is already sorted, the global minimum can ONLY be among the current "front" elements of each array.

                
                

Arrays:

Array 1: [1, 4, 7, 10]  → Currently at: 1

Array 2: [2, 5, 8]      → Currently at: 2

Array 3: [3, 6, 9]      → Currently at: 3



Min-Heap: [1, 2, 3] ← Only 3 elements!



Process:

1. Pop 1 → Add next from Array 1: 4

   Heap: [2, 3, 4]

2. Pop 2 → Add next from Array 2: 5

   Heap: [3, 4, 5]

3. Pop 3 → Add next from Array 3: 6

   Heap: [4, 5, 6]

...continues...
                

                
                
Why this works:

                
    
                    
  • Heap size stays at O(k) where k = number of arrays
    • 
                    
  • We use the sorted property - next min is always at a "front"
    • 
                    
  • Time: O(n×m log k) - much better!
    • 
                

            


            

                
📊 Comparison

                

                    

                        Approach
                        Space
                        Time
                    

                    

                        ❌ Dump all elements
                        O(n×m)
                        O(n×m log(n×m))
                    

                    

                        ✓ One element per array
                        O(k)
                        O(n×m log k)
                    

                

                
Example: 100 arrays, each with 1000 elements

                
    
                    
  • Bad approach: Heap of 100,000 elements
    • 
                    
  • Smart approach: Heap of 100 elements (max)
    • 
                

            


            

                
💭 Remember:

                

                    The arrays are already sorted → Use that! Track only the "front" of each array in the heap, not all elements.
                

            

        

    









    

        
8. Binary Trees (7 Questions)

        
Core traversals and structural checks on binary trees

    


    

        

            Inorder Traversal (LC 94)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the inorder traversal of its nodes' values (Left → Root → Right).

            
Pattern: DFS / recursion or iterative stack — visit left subtree, then node, then right subtree.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,null,2,3]
Output: [1,3,2]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List inorder(TreeNode root) {
    List res = new ArrayList<>();
    dfsInorder(root, res);
    return res;
}
void dfsInorder(TreeNode node, List res) {
    if (node == null) return;
    dfsInorder(node.left, res);
    res.add(node.val);
    dfsInorder(node.right, res);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            Preorder Traversal (LC 144)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the preorder traversal (Root → Left → Right).

            
Pattern: DFS — process node before children.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,null,2,3]
Output: [1,2,3]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List preorder(TreeNode root) {
    List res = new ArrayList<>();
    dfsPreorder(root, res);
    return res;
}
void dfsPreorder(TreeNode node, List res) {
    if (node == null) return;
    res.add(node.val);
    dfsPreorder(node.left, res);
    dfsPreorder(node.right, res);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            Postorder Traversal (LC 145)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the postorder traversal (Left → Right → Root).

            
Pattern: DFS — visit both children before the node.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,null,2,3]
Output: [3,2,1]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List postorder(TreeNode root) {
    List res = new ArrayList<>();
    dfsPostorder(root, res);
    return res;
}
void dfsPostorder(TreeNode node, List res) {
    if (node == null) return;
    dfsPostorder(node.left, res);
    dfsPostorder(node.right, res);
    res.add(node.val);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            Balanced Binary Tree (LC 110)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Determine if a binary tree is height-balanced: for every node, |leftHeight − rightHeight| ≤ 1.

            
Pattern: DFS — return height; if any subtree imbalance, propagate failure.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: true

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isBalanced(TreeNode root) {
    return height(root) != -1;
}
int height(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) return 0;
    int L = height(node.left), R = height(node.right);
    if (L == -1 || R == -1 || Math.abs(L - R) > 1) return -1;
    return 1 + Math.max(L, R);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            Same Tree (LC 100)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the roots of two binary trees, return true if they are structurally identical and have the same values.

            
Pattern: DFS — compare roots and recurse on both children.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: p = [1,2,3], q = [1,2,3]
Output: true

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSameTree(TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    if (p == null && q == null) return true;
    if (p == null || q == null || p.val != q.val) return false;
    return isSameTree(p.left, q.left) && isSameTree(p.right, q.right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            Subtree of Another Tree (LC 572)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given roots of trees root and subRoot, return true if subRoot equals a subtree of root.

            
Pattern: DFS on root; at each node, check isSameTree with subRoot.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2], subRoot = [4,1,2]
Output: true

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSubtree(TreeNode root, TreeNode subRoot) {
    if (root == null) return false;
    if (isSameTree(root, subRoot)) return true;
    return isSubtree(root.left, subRoot) || isSubtree(root.right, subRoot);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            LCA of BST (LC 235)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a BST and two nodes p and q, return their lowest common ancestor.

            
Pattern: BST property — if both values smaller, go left; if both larger, go right; else current node is LCA.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [6,2,8,0,4,7,9], p = 2, q = 8
Output: 6

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode lowestCommonAncestorBST(TreeNode root, TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    if (p.val < root.val && q.val < root.val) return lowestCommonAncestorBST(root.left, p, q);
    if (p.val > root.val && q.val > root.val) return lowestCommonAncestorBST(root.right, p, q);
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(h) | Space: O(h)

        

    









    

        
9. BFS — Breadth First Search (9 Questions)

        
BFS in trees is usually done using a Queue — level order, views, and symmetry

    


    

        

            1. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (LC 102)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal (left to right, level by level). Print nodes level by level.

            
Pattern: BFS with queue — process one level at a time using queue size snapshot.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[9,20],[15,7]]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List> levelOrder(TreeNode root) {
    List> res = new ArrayList<>();
    if (root == null) return res;
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        List level = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            TreeNode n = q.poll();
            level.add(n.val);
            if (n.left != null) q.offer(n.left);
            if (n.right != null) q.offer(n.right);
        }
        res.add(level);
    }
    return res;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    

        

            2. Average of Levels in Binary Tree (LC 637)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Return the average value of nodes at each depth level.

            
Pattern: BFS level order — sum values per level, divide by count.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [3.0, 14.5, 11.0]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List averageOfLevels(TreeNode root) {
    List res = new ArrayList<>();
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        long sum = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            TreeNode n = q.poll();
            sum += n.val;
            if (n.left != null) q.offer(n.left);
            if (n.right != null) q.offer(n.right);
        }
        res.add(sum * 1.0 / sz);
    }
    return res;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            3. Level Order Successor of a Node
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a binary tree and a target node, return the next node visited in level-order (BFS) traversal after the target.

            
Pattern: BFS — when dequeuing target, next polled node (or next in same level batch) is successor; handle last node as null.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: tree with root 1, target = 9 (left of 3)
Output: node 20 (next in level order)

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode levelOrderSuccessor(TreeNode root, TreeNode target) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    boolean found = false;
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            TreeNode n = q.poll();
            if (found) return n;
            if (n == target) found = true;
            if (n.left != null) q.offer(n.left);
            if (n.right != null) q.offer(n.right);
        }
    }
    return null;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            4. Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal (LC 103)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Return zigzag level order — alternate left→right and right→left per level.

            
Pattern: BFS — reverse every other level (or use deque / flag).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[20,9],[15,7]]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List> zigzagLevelOrder(TreeNode root) {
    List> res = new ArrayList<>();
    if (root == null) return res;
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    boolean leftToRight = true;
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        LinkedList level = new LinkedList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            TreeNode n = q.poll();
            if (leftToRight) level.addLast(n.val); else level.addFirst(n.val);
            if (n.left != null) q.offer(n.left);
            if (n.right != null) q.offer(n.right);
        }
        res.add(level);
        leftToRight = !leftToRight;
    }
    return res;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            5. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal II (LC 107)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Return the bottom-up level order traversal (leaf level first).

            
Pattern: BFS top-down, then reverse result list.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[15,7],[9,20],[3]]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List> levelOrderBottom(TreeNode root) {
    List> res = levelOrder(root);
    Collections.reverse(res);
    return res;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            6. Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node (LC 116)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Connect each node to its next right node on the same level; next of rightmost is null.

            
Pattern: BFS level order — link nodes in current level while enqueueing next.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: perfect binary tree
Output: next pointers wired horizontally per level

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
Node connect(Node root) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            Node n = q.poll();
            if (i < sz - 1) n.next = q.peek();
            if (n.left != null) q.offer(n.left);
            if (n.right != null) q.offer(n.right);
        }
    }
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            7. Binary Tree Right Side View (LC 199)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Return values of nodes visible from the right side (last node per level).

            
Pattern: BFS — keep last value of each level.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,null,5,null,4]
Output: [1,3,4]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List rightSideView(TreeNode root) {
    List res = new ArrayList<>();
    if (root == null) return res;
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            TreeNode n = q.poll();
            if (i == sz - 1) res.add(n.val);
            if (n.left != null) q.offer(n.left);
            if (n.right != null) q.offer(n.right);
        }
    }
    return res;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            8. Cousins in Binary Tree (LC 993)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given root and two nodes x and y, return true if they are cousins (same depth, different parents).

            
Pattern: BFS level order — track parent and depth per node.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4], x = 4, y = 3
Output: false

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isCousins(TreeNode root, int x, int y) {
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root);
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        int sz = q.size();
        Set vals = new HashSet<>();
        Set parents = new HashSet<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
            TreeNode n = q.poll();
            if (n.left != null) { q.offer(n.left); if (n.left.val == x || n.left.val == y) parents.add(n.val); vals.add(n.left.val); }
            if (n.right != null) { q.offer(n.right); if (n.right.val == x || n.right.val == y) parents.add(n.val); vals.add(n.right.val); }
        }
        if (vals.contains(x) && vals.contains(y)) return parents.size() == 1;
    }
    return false;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            9. Symmetric Tree (LC 101)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Check if a binary tree is a mirror of itself (symmetric around center).

            
Pattern: BFS — enqueue pairs (left, right) and compare; or DFS mirror check.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,2,3,4,4,3]
Output: true

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSymmetric(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return true;
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>();
    q.offer(root.left); q.offer(root.right);
    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
        TreeNode a = q.poll(), b = q.poll();
        if (a == null && b == null) continue;
        if (a == null || b == null || a.val != b.val) return false;
        q.offer(a.left); q.offer(b.right);
        q.offer(a.right); q.offer(b.left);
    }
    return true;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    









    

        
10. DFS — Depth First Search (15 Questions)

        
DFS usually uses recursion or a stack — paths, construction, and tree properties

    


    

        

            10. Diameter of Binary Tree (LC 543)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Return the length of the diameter — longest path between any two nodes (in edges).

            
Pattern: DFS — at each node compute leftHeight + rightHeight; track global max.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5]
Output: 3

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int diameterOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) {
    int[] max = new int[1];
    heightForDiameter(root, max);
    return max[0];
}
int heightForDiameter(TreeNode node, int[] max) {
    if (node == null) return 0;
    int L = heightForDiameter(node.left, max);
    int R = heightForDiameter(node.right, max);
    max[0] = Math.max(max[0], L + R);
    return 1 + Math.max(L, R);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            11. Invert Binary Tree (LC 226)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Invert the tree by swapping left and right children at every node.

            
Pattern: DFS or BFS — swap children recursively.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [4,2,7,1,3,6,9]
Output: [4,7,2,9,6,3,1]

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode invertTree(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    TreeNode tmp = root.left;
    root.left = invertTree(root.right);
    root.right = invertTree(tmp);
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            12. Maximum Depth of Binary Tree (LC 104)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Return the maximum depth (height) of the tree.

            
Pattern: DFS — 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: 3

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int maxDepth(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return 0;
    return 1 + Math.max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right));
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            13. Convert Sorted Array to BST (LC 108)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Convert a sorted array into a height-balanced BST.

            
Pattern: DFS — pick mid as root, recurse on left and right halves.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [-10,-3,0,5,9]
Output: balanced BST

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode sortedArrayToBST(int[] nums) {
    return build(nums, 0, nums.length - 1);
}
TreeNode build(int[] a, int lo, int hi) {
    if (lo > hi) return null;
    int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;
    TreeNode root = new TreeNode(a[mid]);
    root.left = build(a, lo, mid - 1);
    root.right = build(a, mid + 1, hi);
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            14. Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List (LC 114)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Flatten the tree to a linked list in preorder using the right pointer; return root.

            
Pattern: DFS postorder-style — flatten left and right, attach to root's right.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,5,3,4,null,6]
Output: 1→2→3→4→5→6 (right pointers)

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
void flatten(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    flatten(root.left);
    flatten(root.right);
    TreeNode right = root.right;
    root.right = root.left;
    root.left = null;
    TreeNode cur = root;
    while (cur.right != null) cur = cur.right;
    cur.right = right;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            15. Validate Binary Search Tree (LC 98)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Determine if the tree is a valid BST (left < root < right for all nodes).

            
Pattern: DFS — pass min/max bounds down the tree.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [2,1,3]
Output: true

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isValidBST(TreeNode root) {
    return valid(root, null, null);
}
boolean valid(TreeNode n, Integer lo, Integer hi) {
    if (n == null) return true;
    if ((lo != null && n.val <= lo) || (hi != null && n.val >= hi)) return false;
    return valid(n.left, lo, n.val) && valid(n.right, n.val, hi);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            16. Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) (LC 236)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Find the lowest common ancestor of two given nodes p and q in a binary tree.

            
Pattern: DFS — if node is p or q or null return it; if both subtrees return non-null, node is LCA.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,5,1,6,2,0,8], p = 5, q = 1
Output: 3

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode root, TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    if (root == null || root == p || root == q) return root;
    TreeNode L = lowestCommonAncestor(root.left, p, q);
    TreeNode R = lowestCommonAncestor(root.right, p, q);
    if (L != null && R != null) return root;
    return L != null ? L : R;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            17. Kth Smallest Element in BST (LC 230)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Return the kth smallest value in the BST (1-indexed).

            
Pattern: Inorder DFS — visit left, node, right; count until k.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,1,4,null,2], k = 1
Output: 1

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int kthSmallest(TreeNode root, int k) {
    int[] count = new int[1];
    int[] ans = new int[1];
    inorderKth(root, k, count, ans);
    return ans[0];
}
void inorderKth(TreeNode n, int k, int[] count, int[] ans) {
    if (n == null) return;
    inorderKth(n.left, k, count, ans);
    if (++count[0] == k) { ans[0] = n.val; return; }
    inorderKth(n.right, k, count, ans);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            18. Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder (LC 105)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Build a binary tree from preorder and inorder traversal arrays.

            
Pattern: DFS — preorder[0] is root; split inorder at root index for left/right subtrees.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: preorder = [3,9,20,15,7], inorder = [9,3,15,20,7]
Output: reconstructed tree

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode buildTree(int[] preorder, int[] inorder) {
    Map idx = new HashMap<>();
    for (int i = 0; i < inorder.length; i++) idx.put(inorder[i], i);
    return build(preorder, 0, preorder.length - 1, inorder, 0, inorder.length - 1, idx);
}
// build helper uses idx map to find root in inorder
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            19. Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree (LC 297)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree to a string and deserialize back.

            
Pattern: DFS preorder with null markers, or BFS level order encoding.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,null,null,4,5]
Output: string → tree round-trip

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
String serialize(TreeNode root) {
    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
    dfsSer(root, sb);
    return sb.toString();
}
void dfsSer(TreeNode n, StringBuilder sb) {
    if (n == null) { sb.append("#,"); return; }
    sb.append(n.val).append(",");
    dfsSer(n.left, sb);
    dfsSer(n.right, sb);
}
TreeNode deserialize(String data) {
    Queue q = new LinkedList<>(Arrays.asList(data.split(",")));
    return dfsDes(q);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            20. Path Sum (LC 112)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Return true if there exists a root-to-leaf path whose node values sum to targetSum.

            
Pattern: DFS — subtract node value; at leaf check remainder is zero.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [5,4,8,11,null,13,4,7,2], targetSum = 22
Output: true

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean hasPathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
    if (root == null) return false;
    if (root.left == null && root.right == null) return root.val == targetSum;
    return hasPathSum(root.left, targetSum - root.val)
        || hasPathSum(root.right, targetSum - root.val);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            21. Sum Root to Leaf Numbers (LC 129)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Each root-to-leaf path forms a number; return the sum of all such numbers.

            
Pattern: DFS — accumulate digits along path; add to total at leaf.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [4,9,0,5,1]
Output: 1026

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int sumNumbers(TreeNode root) {
    return dfsSum(root, 0);
}
int dfsSum(TreeNode n, int cur) {
    if (n == null) return 0;
    cur = cur * 10 + n.val;
    if (n.left == null && n.right == null) return cur;
    return dfsSum(n.left, cur) + dfsSum(n.right, cur);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            22. Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum (LC 124)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Return the maximum path sum anywhere in the tree (path need not include root).

            
Pattern: DFS — for each node, max downward gain; update global with leftGain + val + rightGain.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [-10,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: 42

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int maxPathSum(TreeNode root) {
    int[] max = new int[]{Integer.MIN_VALUE};
    gain(root, max);
    return max[0];
}
int gain(TreeNode n, int[] max) {
    if (n == null) return 0;
    int L = Math.max(0, gain(n.left, max));
    int R = Math.max(0, gain(n.right, max));
    max[0] = Math.max(max[0], n.val + L + R);
    return n.val + Math.max(L, R);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            23. Path Exists from Root to Leaf (LC 112)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Basic path existence: is there a root-to-leaf path with the given sum?

            
Pattern: DFS — same as Path Sum; check remainder at leaf.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3], target = 3
Output: true (path 1→2)

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean pathExistsRootToLeaf(TreeNode root, int target) {
    return hasPathSum(root, target);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    

        

            24. Path Exists at Any Nodes (LC 437)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Return the number of paths where sum equals target; path can start/end at any node (not only root-to-leaf).

            
Pattern: DFS with prefix-sum map — count paths with running sum.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [10,5,-3,3,2,null,11,3,-2,null,1], targetSum = 8
Output: 3

        

        

            
Animation

            
Placeholder — animation to be added later.

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int pathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
    Map map = new HashMap<>();
    map.put(0L, 1);
    return dfsPathSum(root, 0L, targetSum, map);
}
int dfsPathSum(TreeNode n, long sum, int target, Map map) {
    if (n == null) return 0;
    sum += n.val;
    int count = map.getOrDefault(sum - target, 0);
    map.put(sum, map.getOrDefault(sum, 0) + 1);
    count += dfsPathSum(n.left, sum, target, map);
    count += dfsPathSum(n.right, sum, target, map);
    map.put(sum, map.getOrDefault(sum, 0) - 1);
    return count;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    









    

        
11. Dynamic Programming (19 Questions)

        
19 DP problems — 1D, knapsack, LCS/LIS, and grid paths

    


    
    

        

            Fibonacci Number (LC 509)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given n, return the nth Fibonacci number where F(0)=0, F(1)=1, and F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2).

            
Reference: LeetCode — Fibonacci Number

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: n = 2
Output: 1

            
Test Case 2:
Input: n = 4
Output: 3

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    int a = 0, b = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        int c = a + b;
        a = b;
        b = c;
    }
    return b;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            Climbing Stairs (LC 70)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Return the number of distinct ways to reach the top (n steps).

            
Reference: LeetCode — Climbing Stairs

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: n = 2
Output: 2

            
Test Case 2:
Input: n = 5
Output: 8

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int climbStairs(int n) {
    if (n <= 2) return n;
    int a = 1, b = 2;
    for (int i = 3; i <= n; i++) {
        int c = a + b;
        a = b;
        b = c;
    }
    return b;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            Counting Bits (LC 338)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given n, return an array ans of length n+1 where ans[i] is the number of 1's in the binary representation of i.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Counting Bits

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: n = 2
Output: [0,1,1]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: n = 5
Output: [0,1,1,2,1,2]

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int[] countBits(int n) {
    int[] dp = new int[n + 1];
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1);
    }
    return dp;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Min Cost Climbing Stairs (LC 746)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Each step has a cost. You can start from step 0 or 1 and climb 1 or 2 steps. Return minimum cost to reach the top.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Min Cost Climbing Stairs

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: cost = [10,15,20]
Output: 15

            
Test Case 2:
Input: cost = [1,100,1,1,1,100,1,1,100,1]
Output: 6

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int minCostClimbingStairs(int[] cost) {
    int n = cost.length;
    int prev2 = cost[0], prev1 = cost[1];
    for (int i = 2; i < n; i++) {
        int curr = cost[i] + Math.min(prev1, prev2);
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = curr;
    }
    return Math.min(prev1, prev2);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            House Robber 1 (LC 198)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Rob non-adjacent houses to maximize money stolen. Return maximum amount without robbing two adjacent houses.

            
Reference: LeetCode — House Robber

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]
Output: 4

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [2,7,9,3,1]
Output: 12

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int rob(int[] nums) {
    int prev2 = 0, prev1 = 0;
    for (int x : nums) {
        int curr = Math.max(prev1, prev2 + x);
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = curr;
    }
    return prev1;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            House Robber 2 (LC 213)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Houses are arranged in a circle (first and last are adjacent). Return maximum money robbed without alerting police.

            
Reference: LeetCode — House Robber II

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [2,3,2]
Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]
Output: 4

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int rob(int[] nums) {
    if (nums.length == 1) return nums[0];
    return Math.max(robLinear(nums, 0, nums.length - 2),
                    robLinear(nums, 1, nums.length - 1));
}
int robLinear(int[] nums, int lo, int hi) {
    int prev2 = 0, prev1 = 0;
    for (int i = lo; i <= hi; i++) {
        int curr = Math.max(prev1, prev2 + nums[i]);
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = curr;
    }
    return prev1;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            Check if There Exists a Subsequence with Sum K
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an array and target K, determine if some subsequence sums exactly to K (elements need not be contiguous).

            
Reference: GeeksforGeeks — Subset Sum (Subsequence Sum)

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: arr = [3, 34, 4, 12, 5, 2], K = 9
Output: true  (4 + 5)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: arr = [3, 34, 4, 12, 5, 2], K = 30
Output: false

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSubsequenceSumK(int[] arr, int K) {
    boolean[] dp = new boolean[K + 1];
    dp[0] = true;
    for (int x : arr) {
        for (int s = K; s >= x; s--) {
            dp[s] = dp[s] || dp[s - x];
        }
    }
    return dp[K];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n·K) | Space: O(K)

        

    


    
    

        

            Perfect Sum Problem
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an array and a target sum, count the number of subsets whose elements sum exactly to the target.

            
Reference: GeeksforGeeks — Perfect Sum Problem

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: arr = [2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 10], target = 10
Output: 3  (e.g. {2,8}, {3,2,5}, {10})

            
Test Case 2:
Input: arr = [1, 1, 1], target = 2
Output: 3

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int perfectSum(int[] arr, int target) {
    int[] dp = new int[target + 1];
    dp[0] = 1;
    for (int x : arr) {
        for (int s = target; s >= x; s--) {
            dp[s] += dp[s - x];
        }
    }
    return dp[target];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n·target) | Space: O(target)

        

    


    
    

        

            01 Knapsack
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given weights and values of items and knapsack capacity W, maximize value. Each item can be taken at most once (0/1).

            
Reference: GeeksforGeeks — 0-1 Knapsack

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: wt = [1,3,4,5], val = [1,4,5,7], W = 7
Output: 9

            
Test Case 2:
Input: wt = [2,3,4,5], val = [3,4,5,6], W = 5
Output: 7

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int knapsack(int[] wt, int[] val, int W) {
    int n = wt.length;
    int[] dp = new int[W + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        for (int w = W; w >= wt[i]; w--) {
            dp[w] = Math.max(dp[w], dp[w - wt[i]] + val[i]);
        }
    }
    return dp[W];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n·W) | Space: O(W)

        

    


    
    

        

            Coin Change 1 (LC 322)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given coin denominations and amount, return the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount, or -1 if impossible.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Coin Change

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: coins = [1,2,5], amount = 11
Output: 3  (5+5+1)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: coins = [2], amount = 3
Output: -1

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int coinChange(int[] coins, int amount) {
    int[] dp = new int[amount + 1];
    Arrays.fill(dp, amount + 1);
    dp[0] = 0;
    for (int a = 1; a <= amount; a++) {
        for (int c : coins) {
            if (c <= a) {
                dp[a] = Math.min(dp[a], dp[a - c] + 1);
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[amount] > amount ? -1 : dp[amount];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(amount·coins) | Space: O(amount)

        

    


    
    

        

            Coin Change 2 (LC 518)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given unlimited coins of each denomination, return the number of combinations that make up the amount.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Coin Change II

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: amount = 5, coins = [1,2,5]
Output: 4

            
Test Case 2:
Input: amount = 3, coins = [2]
Output: 0

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int change(int amount, int[] coins) {
    int[] dp = new int[amount + 1];
    dp[0] = 1;
    for (int c : coins) {
        for (int a = c; a <= amount; a++) {
            dp[a] += dp[a - c];
        }
    }
    return dp[amount];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(amount·coins) | Space: O(amount)

        

    


    
    

        

            Rod Cutting Problem
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given prices for rod lengths 1..n, cut a rod of length n to maximize total revenue.

            
Reference: GeeksforGeeks — Rod Cutting

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: price = [1,5,8,9,10,17,17,20], n = 8
Output: 22

            
Test Case 2:
Input: price = [3,5,8,9,10], n = 5
Output: 16

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int cutRod(int[] price, int n) {
    int[] dp = new int[n + 1];
    for (int len = 1; len <= n; len++) {
        for (int cut = 1; cut <= len; cut++) {
            dp[len] = Math.max(dp[len], price[cut - 1] + dp[len - cut]);
        }
    }
    return dp[n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Longest Common Subsequence (LC 1143)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Longest Common Subsequence

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: text1 = "abcde", text2 = "ace"
Output: 3  ("ace")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: text1 = "abc", text2 = "abc"
Output: 3

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int longestCommonSubsequence(String text1, String text2) {
    int m = text1.length(), n = text2.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[m + 1][n + 1];
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            if (text1.charAt(i - 1) == text2.charAt(j - 1)) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1;
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i - 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[m][n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m·n) | Space: O(m·n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Longest Palindromic Subsequence (LC 516)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, return the length of the longest palindromic subsequence.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Longest Palindromic Subsequence

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "bbbab"
Output: 4  ("bbbb")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "cbbd"
Output: 2  ("bb")

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int longestPalindromeSubseq(String s) {
    int n = s.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[n][n];
    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        dp[i][i] = 1;
        for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i + 1][j - 1] + 2;
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i + 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[0][n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n²)

        

    


    
    

        

            Shortest Common Supersequence (LC 1092)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Return the shortest string that has both str1 and str2 as subsequences.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Shortest Common Supersequence

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: str1 = "abac", str2 = "cab"
Output: "cabac"

            
Test Case 2:
Input: str1 = "abc", str2 = "abc"
Output: "abc"

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
String shortestCommonSupersequence(String str1, String str2) {
    int m = str1.length(), n = str2.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[m + 1][n + 1];
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            if (str1.charAt(i - 1) == str2.charAt(j - 1)) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1;
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i - 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    // Reconstruct using LCS length = dp[m][n]
    // Length of SCS = m + n - dp[m][n]
    return buildSCS(str1, str2, dp);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m·n) | Space: O(m·n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Longest Increasing Subsequence (LC 300)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Longest Increasing Subsequence

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]
Output: 4  ([2,3,7,101])

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [0,1,0,3,2,3]
Output: 4

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int lengthOfLIS(int[] nums) {
    int n = nums.length;
    int[] dp = new int[n];
    Arrays.fill(dp, 1);
    int best = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
            if (nums[j] < nums[i]) {
                dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1);
            }
        }
        best = Math.max(best, dp[i]);
    }
    return best;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Maximum Length of Pair Chain (LC 646)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given pairs [a,b] where a < b, find the longest chain such that (a,b) can follow (c,d) if b < c.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Maximum Length of Pair Chain

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: pairs = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]]
Output: 2

            
Test Case 2:
Input: pairs = [[1,2],[7,8],[4,5]]
Output: 3

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int findLongestChain(int[][] pairs) {
    Arrays.sort(pairs, (a, b) -> a[1] - b[1]);
    int count = 1;
    int end = pairs[0][1];
    for (int i = 1; i < pairs.length; i++) {
        if (pairs[i][0] > end) {
            count++;
            end = pairs[i][1];
        }
    }
    return count;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            Unique Paths (LC 62)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Robot on m×n grid top-left to bottom-right. Move only right or down. Return number of unique paths.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Unique Paths

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: m = 3, n = 7
Output: 28

            
Test Case 2:
Input: m = 3, n = 2
Output: 3

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int uniquePaths(int m, int n) {
    int[] dp = new int[n];
    Arrays.fill(dp, 1);
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
            dp[j] += dp[j - 1];
        }
    }
    return dp[n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m·n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Unique Paths 2 (LC 63)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Grid with obstacles (1 = blocked, 0 = empty). Return unique paths from top-left to bottom-right moving only right or down.

            
Reference: LeetCode — Unique Paths II

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: obstacleGrid = [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]
Output: 2

            
Test Case 2:
Input: obstacleGrid = [[0,1],[0,0]]
Output: 1

        

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int uniquePathsWithObstacles(int[][] grid) {
    int m = grid.length, n = grid[0].length;
    if (grid[0][0] == 1 || grid[m - 1][n - 1] == 1) return 0;
    int[] dp = new int[n];
    dp[0] = 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            if (grid[i][j] == 1) {
                dp[j] = 0;
            } else if (j > 0) {
                dp[j] += dp[j - 1];
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m·n) | Space: O(n)

Rules

        
Simple habits that make this guide and your practice actually stick

    

    
    
            
  • 
                
    Understand, then code
    
                
    Read the problem and intuition before opening a solution. If you cannot explain the approach in plain English, you are not ready to memorize code.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    One topic at a time
    
                
    Finish a pattern section (e.g. sliding window) before jumping to the next. Depth beats skimming ten techniques in one day.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Log what you solve
    
                
    Use the practice tracker on the right (desktop) to record daily counts. Backfill missed days if you forgot—consistency matters more than perfection.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Re-solve without looking
    
                
    After watching a video or reading a solution, close everything and solve again from scratch within 48 hours.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Time yourself lightly
    
                
    Easy: ~20 min, Medium: ~35 min, Hard: ~45 min. If you are stuck past that, read hints—do not grind endlessly on day one.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Random practice
    
                
    Select topics in the nav and use Random Question to mimic interview unpredictability instead of only solving in list order.
    
            
    • 
        







    

        
Did you know

        
Small facts that connect patterns across this guide

    

    
    
            
  • 
                
    Sliding window = fixed budget
    
                
    Many window problems are really “keep a valid range and shrink or grow it”—the same idea as limiting sum, distinct characters, or max frequency.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Prefix sum turns ranges into O(1)
    
                
    Once you have prefix sums, any subarray sum query is two pointer lookups. Pair with a hash map for “sum equals K” style problems.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Binary search is not only for sorted arrays
    
                
    Answer-space binary search works on monotonic predicates too—minimize maximum, capacity, days to ship, etc.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Monotonic stack = next greater/smaller
    
                
    Each element is pushed and popped once—O(n) for histogram, temperatures, and daily temperatures style problems.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    Tree BFS vs DFS
    
                
    BFS uses a queue for level order and shortest path in unweighted trees; DFS uses recursion or a stack for paths, structure, and backtracking.
    
            
    • 
            
  • 
                
    DP = overlapping subproblems
    
                
    If brute force recalculates the same states, tabulation or memoization usually applies—start from the recurrence, then optimize space.
    
            
    •
